title: Signal-to-Noise Ratio Over Time
concept: As diffusion progresses, the ratio of original signal to added noise steadily decreases, tracking exactly how corrupted the image has become.

exercises.prompt: When you slowly add drops of black ink into a glass of clear water, what happens to the water's original clearness over time?

options.text: It fades away more and more as more ink is added
It stays exactly the same no matter what
It suddenly disappears all at once on the first drop

exercises.explain: Just like ink slowly overtakes clear water, noise slowly overtakes the original image signal, drop by drop (step by step).

cards.title: Losing the Signal
cards.body: Ever listened to a radio station while driving through a tunnel? The music (signal) gets weaker while static (noise) gets louder until you can't hear the song anymore. Diffusion models do this to images on purpose!

cards.title: What is Signal-to-Noise Ratio?
cards.body: Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) compares how much of the ORIGINAL image is left versus how much RANDOM NOISE has been added. At the start, SNR is high (mostly original image). As diffusion steps go on, SNR keeps dropping until it's almost all noise.

cards.title: The Fading Microphone
cards.body: Imagine a singer performing (the signal) while a crowd slowly starts cheering louder and louder (the noise). At first you hear the singer clearly. Little by little the cheering drowns out the voice, until all you hear is crowd noise. That's exactly how SNR falls during diffusion.

cards.title: Watching a Photo Fade
cards.body: Take a clear photo of a cat. At step 1, it's 95% cat, 5% noise — you can still see whiskers. By step 50, it might be 50% cat, 50% noise — blurry but recognizable. By step 1000, it's 1% cat, 99% noise — pure static. The SNR number shrinks the whole way down.

cards.title: Myth: Noise Jumps In All at Once
cards.body: Myth: The image goes from perfectly clear to total static in one giant jump. Truth: Noise is added gradually in tiny steps, so the SNR decreases smoothly and predictably — like a slow fade, not a light switch.

cards.title: High SNR vs Low SNR
cards.body: Compare the two ends of the diffusion timeline — one is mostly picture, the other is mostly static.

left.text: Mostly original image, just a little grainy noise sprinkled on top.

right.text: Almost entirely random static, with barely any trace of the original image left.

exercises.prompt: What happens to the Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) as diffusion steps increase?

options.text: It jumps randomly up and down

exercises.explain: As more noise is added at each step, the original signal makes up less and less of the image, so SNR steadily drops.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Near the final diffusion step, the image has a very low Signal-to-Noise Ratio.
exercises.explain: True! By the final steps, the image is mostly noise, meaning very little original signal remains — a very low SNR.

exercises.sentence: As diffusion progresses, the Signal-to-Noise Ratio steadily ____ as more noise is added.
exercises.explain: The ratio decreases because noise keeps piling up while the original signal stays fixed, so noise takes over more and more.

exercises.prompt: Which image most likely came from an EARLY diffusion step (high SNR)?

options.text: A photo that's clearly recognizable with just a light grainy texture
A photo that's almost pure random static with no shapes visible
A photo that's 90% static with only a faint blur of color left

exercises.explain: High SNR means mostly signal (original image) with just a bit of noise — so a clearly recognizable, lightly grainy photo fits an early step.

exercises.prompt: A diffusion model has 1000 total steps. At step 900 (near the end), what would you expect the Signal-to-Noise Ratio to look like compared to step 10 (near the beginning)?

options.text: Step 900 has a much higher SNR than step 10
Step 900 has a much lower SNR than step 10
Both steps have exactly the same SNR
SNR doesn't relate to step number at all

exercises.explain: Since noise keeps building up with each step, step 900 (near the end) has far less original signal remaining than step 10 (near the beginning), so its SNR is much lower.
